title: Ballistic Spatial-Kinetic DNA Sequencing: A Theoretical Framework for Grazing-Angle Cleavage and SWCNT Array Detection
author: Ribeiro Toloczko, Fabrício
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Current Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS) and third-generation single-molecule platforms rely primarily on biochemical processes, enzymatic kinetics, or stochastic nanopore translocations. These methods inherently face limitations regarding speed, reagent costs, and signal-to-noise ratios. This paper proposes a radical departure from biochemical sequencing by introducing a purely physical, solid-state paradigm:  Ballistic Spatial-Kinetic DNA Sequencing . By accelerating a linearized DNA molecule in a vacuum and directing it at a grazing angle against a sub-nanometer cleavage mesh, the polymer undergoes mechanical dissociation. The grazing incidence geometrically amplifies the inter-nucleotide spatial resolution, allowing individual bases to land sequentially across a highly dense, addressable crossbar array of Single-Walled Carbon Nanotubes (SWCNTs). Each nanotube acts as an independent Field-Effect Transistor (FET), identifying the nucleobase via its unique electronic signature. This paper outlines the physical principles, the geometric proof of concept, and the hardware architecture required to materialize this ultra-fast, chemistry-free sequencing mechanism.
